name: Cobb & Mifflin
short_description: Public rockhounding site in southwestern Wisconsin's Driftless Area featuring quartz and agate specimens. A beginner-friendly location with easy terrain access year-round.
description:
Cobb & Mifflin is a public rockhounding location in Iowa County, Wisconsin, situated within the Driftless Area of southwestern Wisconsin. This region escaped glaciation during the last ice age, preserving unique geological features that make it attractive for recreational rockhounding. The site offers access to quartz-rich materials and secondary agate deposits typical of the Upper Midwest.

The geology here reflects the Ordovician and Silurian bedrock exposed throughout the Driftless Area. These paleozoic rocks have weathered over millions of years, creating surface deposits of quartz nodules, agate fragments, and chert pieces that collectors can search. The area's karst topography and spring-fed streams have naturally concentrated these materials in accessible locations.

Visitors to Cobb & Mifflin can expect to work relatively shallow deposits and explore streambanks and hillside outcrops typical of the region. The terrain is manageable for most skill levels, making this an excellent choice for families and newcomers to rockhounding. Material quality varies, but patient searching often yields attractive specimens suitable for display or cutting.

The best collecting occurs after seasonal rains when erosion has freshly exposed new material. Local rockhounders recommend focusing on stream washes and erosion gullies where material naturally accumulates. The site's proximity to Mineral Point and other historic mining areas in the region adds to its geological significance.
history: The Cobb & Mifflin area sits within the Driftless Area, a region where Ordovician limestone and shale bedrock has been exposed and weathered for millions of years following the retreat of glaciers that surrounded but did not cover this landscape. Lead and zinc mining in nearby areas during the 19th century established the region's reputation for mineral wealth, though this particular location has primarily served recreational rockhounders seeking quartz and agate specimens.
tips: Search streambanks and erosion washes where water action has concentrated lighter materials and exposed new specimens. Use a rock hammer and cold chisel to carefully extract quartz and agate nodules from clay and soil without damaging surrounding formations. Look for milky-white to translucent quartz with hints of color, and examine chert pieces for banding patterns that indicate higher-quality agate material. Bring a sifter or screen to separate smaller specimens from sediment when working productive areas.
terrain_notes: Gently rolling terrain typical of the Driftless Area with elevation around 1100 feet. Hillsides feature mixed deciduous forest and open areas with exposed limestone outcropping. Walking surfaces include grassy fields, light woodland, and streamside areas with uneven footing. Most productive areas are within easy walking distance (under 0.5 miles) from typical parking zones. Terrain is wet and muddy during spring snowmelt and after heavy rains.
best_season: April - June and September - November. Spring offers fresh erosional exposure after snowmelt, while fall provides dry conditions ideal for streambank work. Winter is possible but frozen ground and snow cover limit access.
written_directions: From Dodgeville, take WI 23 east toward Mineral Point for approximately 8 miles. Turn north on County Road (follow local signs toward Town of Mifflin). Continue approximately 3 miles on county roads to the Cobb & Mifflin area. Parking is available near the junction; confirm current public access at the Town of Mifflin office or local rockhounding clubs.
road_conditions: County roads are paved but narrow with occasional washboard sections. Passenger vehicles can access main parking areas under normal conditions.
parking_notes: Limited pullout parking available near the main collecting area. No developed lot facilities. Space for approximately 3-5 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Collect reasonable quantities for hobby purposes. Respect private property boundaries - confirm access permissions with local landowners or town officials. No commercial sales of collected material.
rules:
Personal use only
Collect for hobby purposes only
Respect property boundaries
Confirm access with town office or landowners
No commercial use or sales
Leave site as found
nearest_services: Dodgeville, approximately 8 miles south, offers gas stations, restaurants, and grocery stores. Mineral Point, approximately 5 miles south, has additional food and supply options.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved streamside paths may be muddy or slippery during wet seasons. No wheelchair accessibility on primary collecting routes. Sturdy footwear recommended for uneven streambank footing.

faq.question: What types of rocks and minerals can I find at Cobb & Mifflin in Wisconsin?
faq.answer: Cobb & Mifflin is known for quartz specimens, agate, chert, and jasper. Most material found here is milky-white to translucent quartz with secondary agate fragments. The site's location in the Driftless Area makes it particularly productive for weathered nodules that have been naturally exposed by erosion.

faq.question: Is Cobb & Mifflin a free rockhounding site or is there a fee?
faq.answer: Cobb & Mifflin is a free public rockhounding location with no admission fee or permit requirement. However, you should confirm current access status with the Town of Mifflin office before your visit, as local regulations or private land boundaries may affect which areas are open to the public.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Cobb & Mifflin?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season is April through June and September through November. Spring offers excellent conditions with fresh erosional exposure following snowmelt, while fall provides dry terrain ideal for streambank collecting. Winter is possible but ground freeze and snow cover limit accessibility.

faq.question: How far is Cobb & Mifflin from Dodgeville and what tools do I need?
faq.answer: Cobb & Mifflin is approximately 8 miles east of Dodgeville, Wisconsin, making it an easy day trip from town. Bring a rock hammer, cold chisel, safety glasses, and a small sack or bucket for specimens. A hand sifter or screen helps separate smaller pieces from sediment when working productive streamside areas.

faq.question: Is Cobb & Mifflin beginner-friendly for someone new to rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Cobb & Mifflin is ideal for beginners. The terrain is easy to moderate, material is accessible from the surface and shallow depths, and the site requires no specialized permits or skills. Families with children ages 6 and up will find productive collecting opportunities without strenuous hiking.

faq.question: Are there other rockhounding sites near Cobb & Mifflin I can visit in the same trip?
faq.answer: Yes. The Mineral Point area, approximately 5 miles south, has additional public rockhounding opportunities within the same Driftless Area geology. Iowa County has a rich mining history with several locations where rockhounders can find quartz, agate, and local mineral specimens in a concentrated region ideal for a full day of collecting.

meta_description: Free public rockhounding near Dodgeville, Wisconsin. Find quartz, agate, and chert in the scenic Driftless Area. Beginner-friendly with easy terrain.
